feb3a79e02bc930b.body: Small business credit is growing fast at 30.09% this year and now makes up 23.12% of all business lending. The government's UDYAM registration and GST data are making tiny businesses easier to check for creditworthiness. But banks struggle to reach these small borrowers directly because there are so many of them.
feb3a79e02bc930b.implication: Build partnerships with finance companies that can find and assess small borrowers, then either lend together or provide funding to those finance companies.
chain: Government systems make small businesses easier to track and verify
Small business lending grows 30% but banks can't reach all these borrowers directly
Banks partner with finance companies who can find small borrowers and either share the lending or fund the finance company

4a56445ae52c687a.body: Banks are growing gold loans by 121% year-over-year and have been growing for 8 straight periods. They're taking customers away from NBFCs because gold prices shot up from 2023 to 2026, making gold-backed loans more attractive to borrowers. Banks can offer better rates and service because they have local branches to check gold quality and store it safely.
4a56445ae52c687a.implication: Banks with many branches should quickly expand their gold loan business to grab market share while gold prices stay high.
chain: Gold prices rose sharply from 2023 to 2026
Higher gold prices let people borrow more money against their gold jewelry
Banks beat NBFCs because they can check and store gold at local branches

010ca380b2d9a684.body: Small business lending is exploding at 30% annual growth, and these tiny businesses now represent 23% of all MSME lending. Banks that built digital systems to assess small businesses using government registration data and GST records are winning big. Banks without these systems have about 2-3 years to catch up before the opportunity window closes.
010ca380b2d9a684.implication: Banks should immediately invest in digital underwriting systems that can analyze UDYAM registrations and GST data to safely lend to small businesses.
chain: Government digitized small business registration and made tax data available
Banks can now assess creditworthiness of tiny businesses they couldn't lend to before
Early-moving banks are capturing fast-growing market share while others get left behind

666b500886d66da0.body: The government's PLI scheme is paying big manufacturers to boost production. These companies are expanding fast and need more suppliers. Basic metals lending grew 17%, chemicals grew 20%, and engineering grew 35%. Banks can lend to these suppliers more safely because they have contracts with large established companies that guarantee payment.
666b500886d66da0.implication: Banks should offer supply chain finance products like invoice discounting to PLI suppliers instead of regular unsecured business loans.
chain: Government pays manufacturers to increase production
Manufacturers expand and sign contracts with more suppliers
Banks can safely lend to suppliers because big companies back the payments

111782e8659e3a41.body: RBI changed the rules in October 2024 to let more housing loans count toward banks' required priority sector lending. This instantly gave some banks extra PSL credit without doing any new lending - housing PSL lending jumped 39.94% year-over-year. Banks with this surprise surplus can now sell PSL certificates to banks that are behind on their targets.
111782e8659e3a41.implication: If your bank got extra PSL credit from the rule change, sell certificates to banks missing their targets - if you're behind on PSL goals, buy certificates from banks with surplus.
chain: RBI rule change let more housing loans count for required priority lending
Some banks suddenly had extra PSL credit without making new loans
Banks with surplus can sell certificates to banks that need to hit their targets

93ce5ef0317a1dc1.body: Traditional infrastructure lending is winding down — some sectors dropped 26% and 44% this year while others grew 74% and 21%, showing the boom-bust cycle is ending. Banks spent the last few years building teams that know how to finance big, long-term projects like roads and power plants. Government renewable energy targets are creating a new pipeline of solar, wind, and green hydrogen projects that need the same type of financing.
93ce5ef0317a1dc1.implication: Banks should move their project finance teams from traditional infrastructure to renewable energy deals.
chain: Traditional infrastructure projects are finishing their building phase
Banks now have experienced teams who know how to finance large projects
Government renewable targets create new solar and wind projects that need the same financing skills

9bef6e4e00965a35.body: Services sector credit is growing at 18.58% per year, much faster than other sectors, and now makes up 28.21% of all bank lending. NBFCs that specialize in services lending are growing even faster at 16.68% to 57.1% as banks start lending to them again after regulatory changes.
9bef6e4e00965a35.implication: Banks should either build direct lending teams for services companies or increase their lending to NBFCs that already serve this fast-growing market.
chain: Services companies need more credit and are growing fast at 18.58% per year
NBFCs that lend to services are recovering and growing at 16-57% as banks lend to them again
Banks can grow by either lending directly to services companies or by lending more money to these specialized NBFCs

84272ac3d4be9404.body: Credit cards are growing 8.15% yearly with 119 million total cards. Public sector banks have only 24% of credit cards but 64% of debit cards, meaning they have millions of existing customers who could get credit cards but don't. Converting existing customers costs much less than finding new ones.
84272ac3d4be9404.implication: Public sector banks should offer credit card upgrades to their existing debit card customers since they already know and trust these customers.
chain: Public sector banks have way more debit card customers than credit card customers
Their existing customers already trust them and have working bank accounts
Converting existing customers to credit cards costs much less than finding brand new customers

395b93576adcd5b5.body: Micro ATMs dropped 7.17% but there are still 1.37 million of them serving rural areas. Rural people still need cash but these areas can't support the cost of full ATMs or bank branches. This creates a gap that smaller, cheaper micro ATMs can fill.
395b93576adcd5b5.implication: Banks should expand their business correspondent networks with micro ATM devices to serve rural cash demand at lower cost than building full branches.
chain: Rural areas still need cash access but full ATMs cost too much to install and maintain
Micro ATMs can provide basic cash services at much lower cost than full ATMs or branches
Banks can serve this rural market by partnering with local business correspondents who operate micro ATMs

a89fc5166d8d7d11.body: Credit card spending in the 'other' category grew 493% in value and 300% in volume this year. This massive jump comes from new payment systems that let people use credit cards for metro rides, bus fares, and quick tap payments at stores. People are switching from cash and debit cards to credit cards for these everyday purchases.
a89fc5166d8d7d11.implication: Banks should rush to get their credit cards working with these new transit and tap-payment systems before competitors grab the customers.
chain: New payment systems are being built that accept credit cards for metro rides and quick store payments
People are trying these systems and switching from cash to credit cards for daily purchases
Banks that connect their cards to these systems first will capture these new spending habits

1d892103e7b06c43.body: Credit card spending is slowing down, which usually happens before credit card loans start shrinking. When people spend less on their cards, they typically pay down their balances over the next few months, causing total credit card debt to drop. This gives banks a few months' head start to see what's coming.
1d892103e7b06c43.implication: Banks should prepare for lower credit card loan growth and adjust their lending plans before the slowdown hits their loan books.
chain: People are spending less money on their credit cards
Lower spending usually leads to people paying down their card balances over the next few months
Banks can spot this early and adjust their credit card lending before loan growth actually slows

e95b14b88fe3441b.body: Small business credit is growing at 30.09% this year and now represents 23.12% of all business lending. The government's UDYAM registration and GST data are making tiny businesses easier to check, and growth in micro and small business lending has accelerated by 23.77 percentage points. Banks find it hard to reach thousands of small borrowers directly, so they need partners who can do the ground work.
e95b14b88fe3441b.implication: Banks should fund NBFCs and fintech lenders who specialize in small business loans, using co-lending agreements or warehouse credit lines, because these partners can reach and assess tiny borrowers that banks cannot serve efficiently on their own.
chain: UDYAM registration and GST data make small businesses formally visible and checkable for the first time
Small business lending is growing at 30.09% because lenders can now verify creditworthiness using this new data
Banks cannot handle thousands of tiny loans directly, so they should finance specialist lenders who do the on-ground work and use the new data to pick good borrowers

147a8a5b0a116cc6.body: Banks are growing gold loans by 121.21 percent compared to last year, and this growth has continued for 8.0 periods in a row. Gold prices jumped between 2023 and 2026, so people who own gold can now borrow much more money against it. Banks are winning customers from finance companies because banks have local branches where staff can check the gold is real and keep it safe, plus banks charge lower interest rates.
147a8a5b0a116cc6.implication: A bank should open or expand gold loan counters at branches, train staff to test gold purity quickly, and advertise lower rates than finance companies to pull in these borrowers while gold stays expensive.
chain: Gold prices rose sharply from 2023 to 2026, making each piece of gold worth much more as collateral
Banks can lend bigger amounts against the same gold and do it more safely than finance companies because they have local branches to verify and store the gold
Borrowers switch to banks for better rates and convenience, driving the 121.21 percent yearly growth that has lasted 8.0 periods

f42bf3fba8db68c6.body: Small business lending is growing at 30.09 pct per year, and these tiny businesses now make up 23.12 pct of all MSME lending. Their growth rate jumped by 23.77 pp recently, reaching 32.68 pct, while bigger MSME borrowers are growing 20.06 pp slower. Banks using government registration systems and GST records to assess these small businesses are capturing this boom.
f42bf3fba8db68c6.implication: Build digital underwriting systems now that use UDYAM registration and GST data to assess small businesses, because you have roughly 2-3 years before this 30.09 pct growth opportunity becomes too competitive.
chain: Government formalization through UDYAM and GST is making tiny businesses visible and assessable for the first time
Banks with digital systems to read this government data can now lend to a huge new pool of 23.12 pct of the MSME market growing at 30.09 pct
Banks without these systems will miss this window as competitors lock in relationships with these fast-growing small borrowers

477e3ce85ab580df.body: The government is paying big manufacturers to expand production under the PLI scheme, and those manufacturers need more suppliers. Lending to suppliers in basic metals grew 16.65 pct and 19.91 pct, chemicals grew 20.6 pct and 19.36 pct, and engineering grew 34.51 pct. These suppliers are safer to lend to because they have contracts with large established companies that guarantee payment.
477e3ce85ab580df.implication: Banks should lend more to suppliers in these industries, especially engineering where growth hit 34.51 pct, because the contracts with big manufacturers reduce the risk of non-payment.
chain: Government pays big manufacturers to expand under PLI, so those manufacturers need more parts and materials from suppliers
Suppliers get contracts with large established companies, which makes them safer borrowers because payment is more certain
Banks can lend more confidently to these suppliers, especially in fast-growing areas like engineering

fc9c6de447bd1342.body: Housing loans that count toward priority sector lending jumped 39.94% year-over-year after RBI raised the ceiling in October 2024. Banks did not lend more money; the rule change simply let them reclassify existing housing loans as priority sector loans. This created instant surplus PSL credits that banks can now sell to other banks who are short on their required priority sector targets.
fc9c6de447bd1342.implication: Buy PSL certificates from banks with reclassification surplus at a discount, because they got these credits for free and will sell cheap to monetize them before other banks flood the market with the same surplus.
chain: RBI raised the housing loan ceiling for priority sector lending in October 2024, so loans that did not count before suddenly counted
Banks with large housing portfolios instantly had extra PSL credits without making any new loans
Banks holding this free surplus will sell PSL certificates quickly and cheaply to turn the windfall into cash

1b1a23061d71b77e.body: Old infrastructure lending is slowing down — some sectors fell -26.03% and -44.06% this year, while new renewable energy sectors jumped 74.02% and 21.47%. Banks already have teams that know how to finance big, long-term projects like roads and power plants. Government renewable energy targets mean solar, wind, and green hydrogen projects now need the same kind of financing.
1b1a23061d71b77e.implication: Banks should move their project finance teams from old infrastructure to renewable energy projects, using the same skills but on new deals.
chain: Traditional infrastructure projects are finishing and paying back loans, so those sectors are shrinking
Government is pushing renewable energy targets, creating new solar, wind, and hydrogen projects that need big, long-term loans
Banks can use their existing project finance teams and skills to lend to these new renewable projects instead

d3892b363fde0d18.body: Services sector lending is growing at 18.58% per year, much faster than most other types of loans, and now represents 28.21% of all bank credit. Banks are increasingly lending to NBFCs that specialize in services loans, and this channel is growing at 16.68% to 57.1% after regulators eased some rules. Some specific services sub-sectors are growing extremely fast — one at 52.75%, another at 40.7%, and another at 27.73%.
d3892b363fde0d18.implication: A bank should build or expand its services lending book, either by lending directly to services companies or by partnering with NBFCs that already know this sector well, because this is where credit demand is strongest right now.
chain: Regulators changed the rules on how banks can lend to NBFCs, making it easier and cheaper again
Banks responded by increasing loans to NBFCs that focus on services companies, and that flow grew 16.68% to 57.1%
Services credit overall is now growing at 18.58% and taking a bigger share of total lending at 28.21%, so banks that enter now can ride this wave

dd846debc78098b5.body: Credit cards are growing at 8.15 percent per year, with 119436129.0 total cards in the market. Public sector banks hold only 23.8 percent of all credit cards, but they control a much larger share of debit cards, meaning they already have millions of customers who trust them for banking but haven't been offered credit cards. Private banks have 71.01 percent of the credit card market, showing public sector banks are missing a huge opportunity with their own customer base.
dd846debc78098b5.implication: Public sector banks should launch simple credit card programs targeting their existing debit card customers, because selling to people who already bank with you costs far less than finding completely new customers.
chain: Public sector banks have many debit card customers but very few credit card customers compared to private banks
These existing customers already trust the bank and have accounts, so offering them credit cards is much cheaper than finding strangers
By converting even a small portion of their own customers, public sector banks can grow their credit card business quickly without heavy marketing costs

830a06c535e38bcf.body: Micro ATMs fell by -7.17 percent, but there are still 1367989.0 of them across rural India. Rural customers need cash, yet full ATMs and branches cost too much to run in villages. That leaves over a million micro ATMs as the only practical way to deliver cash in these areas.
830a06c535e38bcf.implication: Banks should partner with business correspondents who run these 1367989.0 micro ATMs, offering cash-management loans and working-capital credit so the network stays alive and reaches customers no branch can serve.
chain: Micro ATMs dropped -7.17 percent but 1367989.0 units still operate in villages where full branches are too expensive
Rural customers have no other way to withdraw cash, so these devices remain the only link to banking
Banks can lend to the agents running these machines, keeping the network funded and expanding rural deposit and loan reach

f9bf1081007c4162.body: Credit card spending in the 'other' category jumped 492.78% in value and 299.83% in volume this year. This explosion comes from people now using credit cards to pay for metro rides, bus tickets, and quick tap purchases at stores—things they used to pay for with cash or debit cards. New payment systems that let you tap your credit card on a metro gate or at a shop counter are making this possible.
f9bf1081007c4162.implication: Banks should push credit cards hard into transit systems and small everyday merchants, because people are ready to switch from cash to cards for these routine purchases.
chain: New tap-to-pay systems are installed in metros, buses, and small shops
People find it easier to tap their credit card than fumble with cash or debit cards
Everyday spending that was never on credit cards before now flows through them, creating huge growth in a new category

fec69dd4b9408c17.body: Small business credit is growing at 30.09 percent this year and now makes up 23.12 percent of all business lending. Growth in micro and small business lending jumped by 23.77 percentage points, driven by government systems like UDYAM registration and GST data that make it easier to verify tiny businesses. Banks struggle to reach thousands of small borrowers on their own, so they need partners who can handle the on-the-ground work of finding and checking these customers.
fec69dd4b9408c17.implication: Banks should build co-lending partnerships and warehouse-financing arrangements with fintech companies and NBFCs that specialize in reaching small businesses, using the new government data infrastructure to underwrite loans the bank couldn't originate directly.
chain: Government UDYAM registration and GST data now make tiny businesses easier to verify and lend to
Small business lending is growing fast at 30.09 percent, but banks can't efficiently reach thousands of scattered small borrowers by themselves
Banks need partners who do the ground work—finding customers, collecting data, initial checks—then share the lending or get funded by the bank

e706f19358f9fa64.body: Banks are growing gold loans by 121.21 percent compared to last year, and this growth has been going up for 10.0 periods in a row. Gold prices jumped sharply between 2023 and 2026, which means customers can now borrow much more money using the same gold jewelry or coins as security. Banks are taking customers away from finance companies because banks have local branches where staff can check the gold is real and store it safely, and banks also charge lower interest rates.
e706f19358f9fa64.implication: Banks should open more gold-loan counters in branches, train staff to test gold quickly, and offer fast approvals to grab market share while gold prices stay high and customers want bigger loans.
chain: Gold prices rose sharply from 2023 to 2026, so the same gold ornament is now worth much more as collateral
Customers can borrow larger amounts against their gold, making gold loans more attractive than before
Banks with local branches and lower rates are winning customers from finance companies, driving 121.21 percent year-on-year growth

9aa6f526d767fd25.body: Small business lending is growing at 30.09 pct per year, and these tiny businesses now make up 23.12 pct of all MSME lending. Their growth rate jumped by 23.77 pp recently, reaching 32.68 pct, while bigger MSME borrowers are growing 20.06 pp slower. Banks are using government registration systems and GST records to check if these small businesses can repay loans, which is why lending to them is taking off.
9aa6f526d767fd25.implication: A bank should invest in systems that connect to UDYAM registration and GST data to assess small businesses, because this group is growing at 30.09 pct per year and now represents 23.12 pct of MSME lending.
chain: Government registration and GST records make it easier to check if tiny businesses can repay loans
Banks can now lend safely to small businesses that were too hard to assess before
Small business lending grows at 32.68 pct while bigger MSME lending grows much slower, creating a 20.06 pp gap

09162e5b2ba67f39.body: The government is paying big manufacturers to expand under the PLI scheme, and those manufacturers need more suppliers. Lending to suppliers in basic metals grew 16.65 pct and 19.91 pct, chemicals grew 20.6 pct and 19.36 pct, and engineering grew 34.51 pct. These suppliers are safer to lend to because they have contracts with large established companies that guarantee payment.
09162e5b2ba67f39.implication: Banks should lend to suppliers in these three sectors because they have guaranteed buyers and the government scheme is creating steady demand for their products.
chain: Government pays big manufacturers to expand production under PLI scheme
Those manufacturers need more suppliers in metals, chemicals, and engineering to meet their production targets
Suppliers get contracts with big buyers which makes them safer to lend to because payment is more certain

4648d554b5f5e48a.body: Housing loans that count toward priority sector lending jumped 39.94% year-over-year after RBI raised the ceiling in October 2024. Banks did not lend more money; the rule change simply let them reclassify existing housing loans as priority sector loans. This created instant surplus PSL credits that banks can now sell to other banks who are short on their required priority sector targets.
4648d554b5f5e48a.implication: A bank should buy priority sector lending certificates from banks with surplus credits, because it is cheaper and faster than originating new priority sector loans to meet regulatory targets.
chain: RBI raised the housing loan ceiling that counts toward priority sector lending in October 2024
Banks reclassified existing housing loans under the new rule, creating a 39.94% jump in reported priority sector housing loans without any new lending
Banks with surplus priority sector credits can now sell certificates to banks that are short, creating a liquid market for meeting regulatory requirements

3a088aa2a3ec90ca.body: Old infrastructure lending is shrinking — some sectors dropped -26.03% and -44.06% this year — while renewable energy sectors jumped 74.02% and 21.47%. Banks already have teams that know how to lend for big, long-term projects like highways and power plants. Government renewable energy targets mean solar, wind, and green hydrogen projects now need the same kind of financing that old infrastructure used to get.
3a088aa2a3ec90ca.implication: The bank should move its project finance teams from old infrastructure into renewable energy lending, using the same skills but on solar, wind, and green hydrogen deals.
chain: Old infrastructure projects are finishing and paying back loans, so those sectors are shrinking fast
Government is pushing hard on renewable energy targets, creating demand for big new solar, wind, and hydrogen projects
Banks can use their existing project finance skills to lend on these new renewable deals instead of waiting for old infrastructure to come back

06ba3344a36705bc.body: Services sector lending is growing at 18.58% per year and now makes up 28.21% of all bank credit. Banks are lending more through NBFCs that focus on services, and this channel grew 16.68% to reach 57.1% of the flow. Three services sub-sectors are exploding: one is growing at 52.75%, another at 40.7%, and another at 27.73% per year.
06ba3344a36705bc.implication: A bank should build or expand its services lending book, either directly or by partnering with NBFCs that specialize in high-growth services sub-sectors, because this is where credit demand is strongest and growing fastest.
chain: Regulators eased some rules on bank lending to NBFCs, making it easier for banks to fund NBFCs that lend to services companies
Services companies need more credit as the economy shifts toward services, so demand is rising much faster than in older sectors like manufacturing
Banks that lend to services directly or through NBFCs can grow their loan book at 18.58% or higher, much faster than the overall market

20ef466781e9503c.body: The credit card market has 119436129.0 cards and is growing at 8.15 percent per year. Private banks control 71.01 percent of all credit cards, while public sector banks hold only 23.8 percent. Public sector banks already have millions of debit card customers who trust them but haven't been given credit cards, so they are missing a huge chance to lend to people they already know.
20ef466781e9503c.implication: Public sector banks should start offering credit cards to their existing debit card customers, because they already have the customer relationships but are letting private banks take 71.01 percent of the credit card business.
chain: Credit cards are growing at 8.15 percent per year, creating more lending opportunities every month
Public sector banks have only 23.8 percent of credit cards but a much bigger share of debit cards, meaning they have millions of customers they aren't offering credit to
Private banks are taking 71.01 percent of the market by offering cards to customers that public banks could easily reach first

71cb0c1b705b33a2.body: There are 1367989.0 micro ATMs in rural India today, down -7.17 percent from last year. Villages need cash but full ATMs and branches are too expensive to build there. These micro ATMs are the only cheap way to get cash to people in remote areas.
71cb0c1b705b33a2.implication: The bank should protect and grow its micro ATM network because it is the only practical way to serve rural customers who need cash.
chain: Micro ATMs dropped -7.17 percent but 1367989.0 still operate across villages
Full ATMs and branches cost too much to run in rural areas so micro ATMs are the only affordable option
Banks that keep their micro ATM networks strong will be the only ones able to serve rural cash needs

f3b2cac71b70bcc8.body: Credit card spending in the 'other' category jumped 492.78% in value and 299.83% in volume this year. People are now tapping their credit cards to pay for metro rides, bus tickets, and quick purchases at stores—things they used to pay for with cash or debit cards. New payment systems let you tap your card on a metro gate or shop counter, making credit cards useful in everyday situations where they were never used before.
f3b2cac71b70bcc8.implication: Banks should issue more credit cards and partner with transit systems and small merchants to install tap-to-pay terminals, because millions of daily commuters and shoppers are switching to credit cards for small purchases.
chain: Cities and stores installed tap-to-pay terminals that accept credit cards for metro rides and quick purchases
People who used to pay cash or debit for these small daily transactions started using credit cards instead because it is faster and more convenient
Credit card spending in this category exploded, creating a huge new market for banks to capture by issuing cards and expanding tap-to-pay acceptance

4b21a8941860844b.body: Personal loans for credit cards and other purposes grew 3.78 percent and 12.7 percent year-on-year, while credit card balances outstanding rose 8.15 percent. Consumer durable loans fell 3.38 percent. Most unsecured lending is expanding, meaning banks are willing to lend more to people without collateral because they see demand and acceptable risk.
4b21a8941860844b.implication: A bank should grow its unsecured personal loan book now, especially in the other personal loan category growing at 12.7 percent, while watching credit quality closely since these loans have no asset backing.
chain: Banks are lending more personal loans without collateral, with growth of 3.78 percent and 12.7 percent in two categories
People are borrowing more on credit cards too, with outstanding balances up 8.15 percent, showing steady demand for unsecured credit
This is a good time to lend in these categories, but the bank must monitor repayment carefully because there is no property or gold to recover if borrowers default

a113da30c86189bf.body: Card terminals grew 1.45 percent, vehicle loans jumped 17.97 percent, but consumer durable loans fell 3.38 percent. People are borrowing heavily to buy cars and trucks, using cards a bit more, yet pulling back on financing fridges and TVs. The pattern says households are spending selectively—big-ticket transport yes, home goods no.
a113da30c86189bf.implication: A bank should push auto finance hard, keep card offers steady, and avoid chasing consumer-durable portfolios until that 3.38 percent decline reverses.
chain: Card infrastructure is growing at 1.45 percent and vehicle lending is surging at 17.97 percent, so people are transacting more and financing expensive transport.
At the same time consumer durable lending dropped 3.38 percent, meaning households are skipping loans for appliances and electronics.
Banks should allocate capital to auto loans where demand is strong and wait on durable-goods credit until appetite returns.

71b82a4c7d2b9ebb.body: Three out of three customer segments are now actively participating in the unsecured lending cycle, where spending drives balances higher and rising balances increase appetite for more credit. All parts of the feedback loop—spending behavior, outstanding balances, and willingness to borrow—are reinforcing each other across the entire customer base. This means the cycle is fully engaged and self-sustaining.
71b82a4c7d2b9ebb.implication: The bank should prepare for accelerating unsecured loan growth by ensuring adequate capital buffers and tightening underwriting standards before the cycle peaks, because when all segments are active the momentum can turn quickly.
chain: Customers across all three segments are spending more on credit, which pushes their outstanding balances up
Higher balances make customers more comfortable taking on additional unsecured debt, creating appetite for new loans
This appetite feeds back into more spending and higher balances, creating a self-reinforcing loop that accelerates growth until something breaks the cycle

5faa2bbd069e86eb.body: Small business credit is growing at 30.09 percent this year and now makes up 23.12 percent of all business lending. Growth in micro and small business lending jumped by 23.77 percentage points, driven by government systems like UDYAM registration and GST data that make it easier to check if tiny businesses are real and creditworthy. Banks cannot reach thousands of small borrowers alone, so they need partners who can do the ground work of finding customers and verifying them.
5faa2bbd069e86eb.implication: The bank should build partnerships with fintech companies and NBFCs that specialize in reaching small businesses, using these partners to handle customer acquisition and initial checks while the bank provides the funding.
chain: Government systems like UDYAM and GST now create digital records for tiny businesses that were previously invisible
These records let lenders verify small borrowers quickly, making micro-lending suddenly viable at scale
Banks lack the staff and local presence to serve thousands of small customers directly, so they must work through specialized partners who handle the on-ground operations

20dcdf1251505420.body: Banks are growing gold loans by 121.21 percent compared to last year, and this growth has been climbing for 10.0 periods in a row. Gold prices jumped sharply between 2023 and 2026, so customers can now borrow much more money using the same gold jewelry or coins as security. Banks are winning customers from finance companies because they have local branches where staff can check the gold is real and store it safely, plus they charge lower interest rates.
20dcdf1251505420.implication: Banks should open more gold loan counters in branches, train staff to test gold quickly, and advertise lower rates to pull customers from finance companies while gold prices stay high.
chain: Gold prices rose sharply from 2023 to 2026, so the same piece of jewelry now backs a bigger loan.
Banks have trusted branches and lower rates, making customers switch from finance companies.
As long as gold stays expensive, banks can lend more per gram and grow this business fast.

0ee963f047ebdbb2.body: Small business lending is growing at 30.09 pct per year, and these tiny businesses now make up 23.12 pct of all MSME lending. Their growth rate jumped by 23.77 pp recently, reaching 32.68 pct, while bigger MSME borrowers are growing 20.06 pp slower. Banks can now check if these small businesses can repay loans using government registration systems and GST records, which is why lending to them is taking off.
0ee963f047ebdbb2.implication: A bank should invest in systems that pull data from UDYAM registration and GST records to approve small business loans quickly, because these borrowers are growing much faster than larger ones.
chain: Government systems like UDYAM registration and GST records now give banks reliable data on tiny businesses that were previously invisible
Banks can now check if these small borrowers can repay loans, so they feel safe lending to them
Small business lending grows much faster than lending to bigger businesses because banks finally have the data they need

f4ba4a82da51e6e1.body: The government is paying big manufacturers to expand factories under the PLI scheme, and those manufacturers need more parts and materials from smaller suppliers. Lending to suppliers in basic metals grew 16.65 pct and 19.91 pct, chemicals grew 20.6 pct and 19.36 pct, and engineering grew 34.51 pct. These suppliers are safer bets because they have contracts with large established companies that guarantee they will get paid.
f4ba4a82da51e6e1.implication: Banks should lend to suppliers who have contracts with PLI manufacturers, especially in engineering where growth hit 34.51 pct, because the big buyer guarantees payment and reduces the risk of default.
chain: Government pays big manufacturers to build new factories under PLI, so those manufacturers need more suppliers for metals, chemicals, and parts
Suppliers get contracts from these big manufacturers, which means guaranteed orders and payment, making them safer to lend to
Banks can grow their loan book quickly by lending to these suppliers, with lower risk because the big manufacturer stands behind the payment

98cda06aac96e2d9.body: Old infrastructure lending is falling fast — some sectors dropped -44.06% and -26.03% this year — while renewable energy sectors jumped 74.02% and 21.47%. Banks already have teams that know how to lend for big, long-term projects like highways and power plants, and those same skills work for solar, wind, and green hydrogen projects. Government renewable energy targets mean these new projects now need the same kind of financing that old infrastructure used to get.
98cda06aac96e2d9.implication: Move your project finance team from shrinking old infrastructure sectors into renewable energy lending, using the same credit skills and long-term loan structures you already know.
chain: Big infrastructure projects built over the past few years are now repaying loans instead of borrowing new money, so those sectors are shrinking
Government has set big renewable energy targets, creating demand for long-term project loans in solar, wind, and hydrogen
Banks can use their existing project finance skills to lend in these fast-growing renewable sectors instead of waiting for old infrastructure to recover

f98e2568c51e5420.body: Services sector lending is growing at 18.58% per year and now represents 28.21% of all bank credit. Banks are increasingly lending through NBFCs that serve services companies — this channel grew 16.68% and now handles 57.1% of the flow. Three services sub-sectors are taking off fast: one is growing at 52.75%, another at 40.7%, and a third at 27.73% per year.
f98e2568c51e5420.implication: The bank should expand its NBFC partnerships that focus on services lending and build direct capabilities in the three fastest-growing services sub-sectors to capture this 18.58% annual growth opportunity.
chain: Services companies need more credit and banks are meeting that demand partly by lending to NBFCs that specialize in services
NBFCs now handle 57.1% of services credit flow because they understand these businesses better and can move faster than traditional banks
Three specific services sub-sectors are growing extremely fast (52.75%, 40.7%, and 27.73%) creating a clear opportunity for banks that can lend directly or through the right NBFC partners

a4554e70ea751ff4.body: India has 119436129.0 credit cards growing at 8.15 percent per year. Private banks own 71.01 percent of all cards, while public sector banks hold only 23.8 percent. Public sector banks already have millions of debit card customers who trust them and know them, but those customers don't have credit cards yet.
a4554e70ea751ff4.implication: Public sector banks should offer credit cards to their existing debit card customers, because they already have the relationship and the customer data to lend safely.
chain: Public sector banks have a huge base of debit card customers who already bank with them and trust them
These customers need credit cards but are currently going to private banks because public banks aren't offering enough cards
Public banks can grow fast by simply giving credit cards to people they already know, instead of letting private banks take that business

0e3dfdf72235d774.body: There are 1367989.0 micro ATMs in rural India today, down -7.17 percent from last year. These machines are the cheapest way to get cash to remote villages where building full branches or ATMs costs too much. Fewer machines means fewer people can get cash easily.
0e3dfdf72235d774.implication: The bank should invest in more micro ATMs or partner with business correspondents to expand rural cash access before competitors fill the gap.
chain: Micro ATMs are shrinking by -7.17 percent even though villages need cash
Fewer machines mean rural customers struggle to access banking and may switch to competitors who serve them better
Banks that add micro ATMs now can lock in loyal rural customers before others do

5f44f19ef1bec841.body: Credit card spending in the 'other' category jumped 492.78% in value and 299.83% in volume this year. People are tapping their cards to pay for metro rides, bus tickets, and quick store purchases—things they used to pay cash or debit for before. New tap-to-pay systems at metro gates and shop counters turned credit cards into an everyday payment tool.
5f44f19ef1bec841.implication: The bank should issue more credit cards and partner with transit systems and small merchants to install tap-to-pay terminals, because customers now want to use credit cards for daily small purchases.
chain: New tap-to-pay terminals appeared at metro stations and small shops, letting people use credit cards for rides and quick buys
Customers switched from cash and debit to credit cards for these everyday payments because tapping is faster and easier
Credit card spending in this category exploded, showing a huge new market for card issuers and payment infrastructure

923216125adddadb.body: Small business credit is growing at 26.18 percent this year and now makes up 23.06 percent of all business lending. Growth in micro and small business lending jumped by 23.77 percentage points, driven by government systems like UDYAM registration and GST data that make it easier to check if tiny businesses are real and creditworthy. Banks cannot reach thousands of small borrowers alone, so they need partners who can do the ground work of finding customers and verifying them.
923216125adddadb.implication: Banks should build partnerships with fintech companies and NBFCs who specialize in finding and checking small borrowers, using co-lending or warehouse financing to fund these loans while the partners handle customer acquisition and verification.
chain: Government systems like UDYAM and GST now create digital records that prove small businesses exist and show their real sales, making them easier to lend to
Small business lending is growing at 26.18 percent because banks can now trust the data, but banks lack the staff and local presence to find and serve thousands of tiny borrowers directly

Banks need local partners who know the customers and can verify them on the ground, then fund those loans through co-lending or warehouse lines while the partner does the hard work of reaching borrowers

2ab720f5efffd7a6.body: Banks are growing gold loans by 105.5 percent compared to last year, and this growth has been climbing for 12.0 periods in a row. Gold prices jumped sharply between 2023 and 2026, so customers can now borrow much more money using the same gold jewelry or coins as security. Banks are winning customers from finance companies because they have local branches where staff can check the gold is real and store it safely, plus they charge lower interest rates.
2ab720f5efffd7a6.implication: The bank should open more gold loan counters in branches, train staff to test gold quickly, and advertise lower rates than finance companies to pull in customers who want bigger loans against their existing gold.
chain: Gold prices rose a lot from 2023 to 2026, so the same necklace or coin is now worth more money.
Because the gold is worth more, banks can lend bigger amounts while still staying safe, and customers want those bigger loans.
Banks with local branches can check and store the gold easily, so they are taking customers away from finance companies and growing fast for 12.0 periods straight.

e37f7f6f9e1af736.body: Small business lending is growing at 32.68 pct per year, and these tiny businesses now make up 23.06 pct of all MSME lending. Their growth rate jumped by 23.77 pp recently, while bigger MSME borrowers are growing much slower—the gap between small and large is 11.8 pp. Banks can now check if these small businesses can repay loans using government registration systems and GST records, which makes lending to them safer and faster.
e37f7f6f9e1af736.implication: A bank should invest in systems that connect to UDYAM registration and GST data so it can quickly approve loans for small businesses that were previously too hard to assess.
chain: Government created digital records (UDYAM registration and GST filings) that show whether a tiny business is real and earning money
Banks can now check these records instantly instead of guessing, so they feel safe lending to small borrowers they ignored before
Small business lending accelerated by 23.77 pp because banks can finally verify creditworthiness at scale

fd4d03fb7f2a15ca.body: The government is paying big manufacturers to build more factories, and those manufacturers need more parts from smaller suppliers. Lending to suppliers in basic metals grew 22.61 pct and 18.43 pct, chemicals grew 22.63 pct and 21.8 pct and 14.2 pct and 7.43 pct, and engineering grew 23.85 pct and 38.27 pct. These suppliers are safer to lend to because they have contracts with large established companies that guarantee payment.
fd4d03fb7f2a15ca.implication: The bank should lend more to small and medium suppliers in metals, chemicals, and engineering who have confirmed orders from big manufacturers expanding under the government scheme.
chain: Government pays big manufacturers to expand factories under PLI scheme
Those manufacturers need more parts so they order from smaller suppliers in metals, chemicals, and engineering
Suppliers with confirmed orders from big companies are safer to lend to because payment is guaranteed

bfa93f7914ff47c6.body: Housing loans that count toward priority sector lending jumped 38.98% year-over-year after RBI raised the ceiling in October 2024. Banks did not actually lend more money; the rule change simply let them reclassify existing housing loans as priority sector loans. This created instant surplus PSL credits that banks can now sell to other banks who are short on their required priority sector targets.
bfa93f7914ff47c6.implication: A bank should buy PSL credits from banks with surplus or sell its own surplus credits if it has reclassified housing loans, because the market now has more supply and prices may be better for both buyers and sellers.
chain: RBI raised the housing loan ceiling that counts toward priority sector lending in October 2024
Banks reclassified existing housing loans under the new ceiling without making new loans, creating a 38.98% jump in reported priority sector housing loans
Banks with surplus credits can now sell them to banks that need to meet their priority sector targets, making the PSL credit market deeper and more liquid

73961874fae4341f.body: Old infrastructure lending is shrinking — some sectors fell -44.06% and -26.03% this year — while renewable energy sectors jumped 74.02% and 21.47%. Banks already have teams that know how to finance big, long-term projects like highways and power plants, and those same skills work for solar, wind, and green hydrogen projects. Government renewable energy targets mean these new projects now need the same kind of financing that old infrastructure used to get.
73961874fae4341f.implication: Banks should move their project finance teams from old infrastructure sectors into renewable energy lending, using the same credit skills they already have.
chain: Old infrastructure projects are finishing and paying back loans, so lending in those sectors is falling fast
Government is pushing hard on renewable energy targets, creating demand for big new solar, wind, and hydrogen projects
Banks can use their existing project finance skills to lend for these new renewable projects instead of waiting for old infrastructure to come back

ac117b025c53f9dd.body: Services sector lending is growing at 20.41% per year and now makes up 28.28% of all bank credit. Banks are lending more through NBFCs that serve services companies — this channel grew 19.71% and is strengthening. Three services sub-sectors are growing extremely fast: one at 54.95%, another at 40.97%, and a third at 33.69% per year.
ac117b025c53f9dd.implication: The bank should increase lending to NBFCs that specialize in services companies, especially those serving the three fastest-growing sub-sectors, because this is where credit demand is surging and the NBFC channel is recovering strongly.
chain: Services companies need more credit and are growing fast, so they are borrowing more from banks and NBFCs
Banks are lending more through NBFCs because rules around bank-to-NBFC lending have become easier after a difficult period
Three specific services sub-sectors are growing much faster than the rest, so NBFCs serving those areas will need the most funding

df42d20b4606e05e.body: India has 120454115.0 credit cards outstanding, growing at 8.47 percent year-on-year. Private banks hold 70.91 percent of these cards, while public sector banks hold only 23.83 percent — even though public banks control about 64 percent of the debit card base. This gap means public banks have millions of existing customers with debit cards who could be upgraded to credit cards at much lower cost than finding brand-new customers.
df42d20b4606e05e.implication: Public sector banks should launch targeted upgrade programs offering credit cards to their existing debit card holders, capturing share in a growing market where they are currently underrepresented.
chain: Public banks have far more debit card customers than their credit card share suggests
Upgrading an existing customer costs less than acquiring a stranger
By offering credit cards to people who already bank with them, public banks can grow faster and cheaper

1ad48ea50e29890a.body: India has 1358241.0 micro ATMs, but the number fell -8.11 percent recently. These small cash machines work in villages and towns where a full bank branch or big ATM costs too much to run. Even though the count dropped, people in these areas still need cash every day, and micro ATMs cost far less to set up than a real branch.
1ad48ea50e29890a.implication: A bank should keep or grow its network of micro ATMs and business correspondents in rural areas because customers there depend on cash and this channel costs much less than building branches.
chain: Micro ATM numbers dropped -8.11 percent to 1358241.0 units, showing some operators pulled back
Rural customers still need daily cash but full branches are too expensive to justify in small towns
Banks that maintain or expand low-cost micro ATM networks can serve this demand profitably where others cannot

1ca17efc378e31c8.body: Credit card spending in the 'other' category jumped 2365.96 percent in value and 714.01 percent in volume year-on-year. This means people are using cards in completely new places — likely transit gates, vending machines, or small merchants that just got tap-to-pay terminals. The growth is so extreme it shows a new habit forming, not just existing users spending more.
1ca17efc378e31c8.implication: Banks should rush to issue cards that work on these new payment rails (transit tap or UPI-linked credit) and sign up the merchants or transit systems rolling out the terminals, because whoever gets in first will own the new spending habit.
chain: New terminals (transit gates, small shops) suddenly accept card taps, creating places people never used cards before
Consumers try it once, find it faster than cash or apps, and keep using it — so volume and value explode
The bank that issues the card or runs the terminal network captures all that new spending and builds the habit before competitors arrive
